statusLine: WTI crude climbed 3.55% in the week ending June 1st to $90.46/bbl as US commercial inventories fell sharply amid critical Red Sea and Persian Gulf maritime threats.
fullAnalysis:
US crude markets showed bullish momentum in the week ending June 1st, with WTI advancing 3.55% to $90.46/bbl as commercial crude inventories dropped by a significant 9.1 million barrels to 365.1 million barrels. This substantial draw, combined with declining gasoline (-2.6 million barrels) and distillate (-2.1 million barrels) stocks, signals tightening domestic supply conditions despite robust US production holding near 13.7 million barrels per day.

The $3.25 discount of WTI to Brent crude reflects persistent regional dynamics, with Brent at $93.71/bbl commanding a premium due to international supply concerns. This spread remains elevated as global shipping risks continue to impact international crude flows, while the Americas benefit from domestic production security and shorter supply chains to major consumption centers.

Critical maritime advisories remain active across key global chokepoints, including ongoing Houthi attacks in the Red Sea and Bab el Mandeb Strait, plus Iranian threats in the Persian Gulf and Strait of Hormuz. While these don't directly impact Americas production, they support higher global crude prices and strengthen the relative position of Western Hemisphere supplies. The Panama Canal continues operating normally, maintaining crucial connectivity between Americas Atlantic and Pacific crude flows.

US retail fuel prices showed mixed signals with gasoline falling slightly to $4.605/gallon but diesel remaining elevated at $5.523/gallon. The Strategic Petroleum Reserve held steady at 81.2 million barrels, providing limited emergency cushion at roughly 4 days of current consumption. Across the broader Americas, production growth in Guyana's Stabroek block and Brazil's pre-salt continues to offset declining Venezuelan output, supporting regional supply security.
keyPoints: WTI crude gained 3.55% week-on-week to $90.46/bbl through June 1st on tight US inventory conditions
US commercial crude stocks fell 9.1 million barrels to 365.1 million barrels, signaling supply tightness
Critical maritime threats persist in Red Sea and Persian Gulf, supporting global crude premiums
WTI trades at $3.25 discount to Brent, reflecting regional supply dynamics
US production steady near 13.7 million bpd while SPR remains low at 81.2 million barrels
Gasoline and distillate inventories both declined, with diesel prices holding above $5.50/gallon
